name: Maasai
description: The Maasai are semi-nomadic pastoralists of the East African Great Rift Valley, spanning both Kenya and Tanzania. Their identity is inseparably bound to cattle — wealth, ceremony, diet, and social status are all measured in livestock. They are organised into age-grades (ilkiama) where warrior morans (ilmoran) form the society's protector class. Their red shukas, elaborate beadwork, and the adamu jumping dance make them among the most recognised cultures on the continent, though their traditions go far deeper than the iconic images.
cultural_highlights: Ilmoran warrior age-grade — young men who protect the community, herd cattle, and undergo multi-year initiation
Enkiama council of elders — governance by consensus among senior men, with women's councils having advisory roles
Enkidongi — the diviners (laibon) who provide spiritual guidance, medicine, and lead ceremonies
Adamu (jumping dance) — the warrior performance where height of jump demonstrates strength and stamina
Enkiama earrings and beadwork — colour-coded beadwork carries specific meaning: red = bravery, white = peace, blue = sky/God

food_traditions.name: Nyama choma (roasted meat)
food_traditions.description: Meat — particularly goat, sheep and beef — forms the core of celebration feasts

food_traditions.name: Milk (fresh and fermented)
food_traditions.description: Maasai diet traditionally centres on cow's milk; oloisuki (fermented milk in a calabash) is a daily staple

food_traditions.name: Blood and milk mixture
food_traditions.description: Enkioshe — fresh cattle blood mixed with milk, consumed during ceremonies and by warriors and new mothers

music_traditions: Throat singing — polyphonic choral tradition performed without instruments, creating complex harmonic textures
Ilkiama ceremonial chants — specific chants for each transition: circumcision, warriorhood, elderhood, marriage
Enkidong bell and rattle — percussion instruments used to accompany cattle movements and ceremonial song
